title: Comprehensive Review & Final Mock Exam
description: The capstone: a high-yield condensed review across all seven FS knowledge areas, a one-page master formula sheet, mixed multi-step worked problems, and a two-week study plan — everything tied together to power the 110-question comprehensive final mock exam.
key_topics: How the real NCEES FS exam works: 110 questions, 5 h 20 min CBT, closed-book with the electronic FS Reference Handbook at Pearson VUE
Condensed cram review of all seven knowledge areas (math/stats, processes, principles/geodesy, computations, mapping/photogrammetry, boundary law, business/ethics)
Dignity (priority) of calls and the hierarchy of boundary evidence
Master formula sheet pulling one key formula from every module
Mixed multi-step problems: traverse → misclosure → adjust → area
Error propagation, leveling, latitude/departure, inverse, area, curves, scale/GSD, H = h − N, grid/ground combined factor
Calculator fluency: DMS, R▸P / P▸R, statistics mode, store-and-recall
Two-week study plan, exam-day logistics, and time budget (~2.9 min/question)
Category proportions of the FS exam (boundary heaviest, then computations)
Final timed full-length mock exam under exam conditions

key_formulas.name: Differential leveling
key_formulas.formula: HI = Elev + BS;  Elev = HI − FS

key_formulas.name: Geoid relationship
key_formulas.formula: H = h − N  (h = ellipsoidal, H = orthometric, N = geoid undulation)

key_formulas.name: Grid ↔ ground (combined factor)
key_formulas.formula: grid = ground · CF;  ground = grid / CF;  CF = SF · EF

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## The capstone: review everything, then take the mock

You have worked through the math/stats, field, computation, geodesy, mapping, boundary, and business modules. This final module does two things: it **condenses the whole course into a high-yield cram review**, and it **powers the 110-question comprehensive final mock exam** that simulates the real test.

## How the real FS exam works

- **110 questions** delivered as a **computer-based test (CBT)** at a **Pearson VUE** center, offered year-round.
- **6-hour appointment:** 2-min nondisclosure agreement · 8-min tutorial · **5 h 20 min of exam time** · a 25-min scheduled break. That leaves roughly **2.9 minutes per question**.
- **Closed-book**, but you get the **electronic NCEES FS Reference Handbook** (current published version 2.x) on screen the entire exam, plus searchable PDF standards (ALTA/NSPS, FEMA, FGCS leveling, GPAS, NSPS Model Standards, USNMAS). **No personal references.**
- **Pass/fail only** — no numeric score. Results in 7–10 business days via MyNCEES; a failing report breaks down performance by area.
- The seven knowledge areas are **not** equally weighted. **Boundary Law & Real Property is the single largest area (19–29 questions)**, followed by **Survey Computations (17–26)** and **Surveying Processes (16–24)**. Treat the FS as a boundary-and-computation exam as much as a math exam.

## How to use this review + mock

Work the **Key Concepts** cram sheet to refresh every area, keep the **master formula sheet** beside you, and grind the **mixed worked examples** — the real exam chains topics (a curve buried inside an area problem, error propagation on a measured area). Then take the **final mock under timed, closed-book conditions** using only the handbook, and review every miss against its category. The mock is built so the category mix mirrors the real exam, so your diagnostic mirrors what NCEES would report.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## Cram sheet — the most-tested facts in every area

### Applied math & statistics (Area 7)
- **Standard error of the mean** σ_m = σ/√n; **sample std dev** σ = √[Σv²/(n−1)].
- **Error propagation:** sum/inverse-type σ_sum = √(Σσᵢ²); a series of n equal measurements σ_series = σ·√n; product (area) σ = √[(B·σ_a)² + (A·σ_b)²].
- **Weights ∝ 1/σ²**; weighted mean = Σ(W·x)/ΣW.
- **Normal curve:** 68 / 95 / 99.7% at 1 / 2 / 3σ. 95% two-tail Z = 1.960.
- **Blunder vs systematic vs random** — only random errors follow the normal curve; systematic errors are removed by correction, blunders by checks/redundancy.

### Surveying processes & methods (Area 1)
- **GNSS:** static (highest accuracy, long sessions), RTK/kinematic (real-time, baseline-limited), **OPUS** (post-process a single static occupation against CORS).
- **Control hierarchy:** horizontal/vertical networks, network design, accuracy standards; occupy from the whole to the part.
- **Field records:** raw data files and field books are the evidence of the work — keep them complete and unaltered.
- **EDM atmospheric correction:** ~10 ppm per 10 °C or per 1 in Hg.

### Surveying principles & geodesy (Area 4)
- **Geoid** = equipotential surface ⟂ to gravity (mean sea level idealized); the **ellipsoid** is the smooth math reference. **H = h − N** (orthometric = ellipsoidal − geoid undulation).
- **Datums:** NAD83 (horizontal), NAVD88 (vertical). **Lambert Conformal Conic** for E–W states, **Transverse Mercator** for N–S states (SPCS).
- **Convergence** (mapping angle) relates geodetic north to grid north.
- Spherical trig for long lines; **spherical excess** for large triangles.

### Survey computations (Area 5)
- **Leveling:** HI = Elev + BS; Elev = HI − FS; ΣBS − ΣFS = ΔElev. **(c & r) ≈ 0.0206·D²** ft (D in thousands of ft); reciprocal leveling cancels it.
- **Inverse:** D = hypot(ΔN, ΔE); Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) reduced to 0–360°.
- **Traverse:** Lat = L·cos Az, Dep = L·sin Az; misclosure = √(ΣLat²+ΣDep²); precision = misclosure/perimeter; **Bowditch** correction ∝ leg length/perimeter.
- **Area by coordinates** (shoelace); **curves** T = R·tan(I/2), L = R·I·(π/180), **PT = PC + L** (add the arc!).
- **Vertical curve:** Y = Y_PVC + g₁x + [(g₂−g₁)/(2L)]x²; turning point x = −g₁L/(g₂−g₁).
- **Volume:** average end area V = L(A₁+A₂)/2; ÷27 for yd³.

### Mapping, GIS & photogrammetry (Area 2)
- **Photo scale** S = f/(H−h); **GSD** = pixel·H/f; **relief displacement** d = r·h/H.
- **Vector vs raster**; map projections, datums, and **metadata** (the data about the data — required for sharing).
- **Contours** never cross; closer spacing = steeper ground. **TIN / DEM / DSM** terrain models.
- Map types: topographic, planimetric, plat, **ALTA/NSPS**, plan-and-profile.

### Boundary law & real property (Area 3 — the heaviest)
- **Dignity (priority) of conflicting calls — controlling order:**
  1. **Senior rights** (the earlier conveyance wins in a sequential split).
  2. **Written intentions of the parties** (the controlling intent in the deed).
  3. **Calls for monuments** — natural (river, tree) over artificial (iron pin), and monuments over courses and distances.
  4. **Calls for direction (bearing)** over distance.
  5. **Calls for distance.**
  6. **Calls for area (quantity)** — least reliable, controls last.
- **Senior/junior rights:** in a **sequential** conveyance the first grantee takes the full called area; the **remainder (junior) parcel absorbs any excess or deficiency**. **Simultaneous** conveyances (a single recorded plat) **prorate** excess/deficiency among lots.
- **PLSS:** township = 36 sections; Section 1 is NE corner, numbered boustrophedon; restore lost corners by **single/double proportionate measurement**; obliterated corners by collateral evidence.
- **Adverse possession:** open, notorious, continuous, hostile, exclusive for the statutory period.
- **Easement** = a non-possessory right to use another's land; **appurtenant** runs with the land and passes with conveyance; created by grant, reservation, implication, necessity, or prescription.
- **Water:** **accretion** (gradual deposit, boundary moves), **avulsion** (sudden, boundary fixed); riparian (rivers) vs littoral (lakes/sea).
- A **warranty deed** guarantees title; a **quitclaim** conveys only whatever interest the grantor has.

### Business concepts & ethics (Area 6)
- **Ethics — NSPS canons / Model Rules:** the surveyor's **first and foremost duty is to public safety and welfare**; practice only within your **area of competence**; sign/seal **only work under your responsible charge**; keep client matters confidential; no misleading advertising.
- **Contracts** need offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, lawful purpose; scope of work and specifications define the deliverable.
- **Liability:** negligence and **errors & omissions (E&O)**; the standard of care is what a reasonable surveyor would do.
- **Safety:** **Risk = Hazard × Probability**; SDS has 16 sections; JSA/JHA documents step-by-step hazards.
- **Economics:** simple/compound interest, present worth, straight-line depreciation D = (C − S)/n.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Master formula sheet — one key formula from every module

Keep this beside you for the mock. Every entry matches the NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.x.

### Math & statistics
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Standard error of mean | `σ_m = σ/√n` |
| Sample std deviation | `σ = √[Σv²/(n−1)]` |
| Error propagation (sum/inverse) | `σ_sum = √(σ₁² + σ₂² + … + σₙ²)` |
| Series of n equal measurements | `σ_series = σ·√n` |
| Area (product) propagation | `σ_A = √[(B·σ_a)² + (A·σ_b)²]` |
| Weighted mean | `M = Σ(W·x)/ΣW`,  `W ∝ 1/σ²` |

### Field measurement & leveling
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Height of instrument | `HI = Elev + BS` |
| New elevation | `Elev = HI − FS` |
| Curvature & refraction (rod) | `(c & r) ≈ 0.0206·D²` ft  (D in 1000s ft) |
| Slope → horizontal | `H = S·cos(zenith) = S·cos(vert. angle)` |
| Tape temperature correction | `ΔL = α·L·(T − T₀)` |

### Traverse & COGO
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Latitude / departure | `Lat = L·cos(Az)`,  `Dep = L·sin(Az)` |
| Inverse distance | `D = hypot(ΔN, ΔE)` |
| Inverse azimuth | `Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN)` → 0–360° |
| Forward coordinates | `N₂ = N₁ + D·cos(Az)`,  `E₂ = E₁ + D·sin(Az)` |
| Linear misclosure | `misclose = √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²)` |
| Relative precision | `precision = misclose / perimeter` |
| Bowditch correction (per leg) | `c_lat = −ΣLat·(legL/perim)` (likewise dep) |

### Area & volume
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Area by coordinates (shoelace) | `A = ½·\|Σ (Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)\|` |
| Acres | `acres = ft² / 43,560` |
| Average end-area volume | `V = L·(A₁ + A₂)/2` |
| Cubic yards | `yd³ = ft³ / 27` |

### Curves
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Degree of curve (arc) | `D = 5,729.58 / R` |
| Tangent | `T = R·tan(I/2)` |
| Curve length | `L = R·I·(π/180)` |
| Stationing | `PC = PI − T`,  `PT = PC + L` |
| Vertical curve elevation | `Y = Y_PVC + g₁·x + [(g₂−g₁)/(2L)]·x²` |
| Turning point from PVC | `x = −g₁·L/(g₂ − g₁)` |

### Photogrammetry & mapping
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Photo scale | `S = f / (H − h)` |
| Ground sample distance | `GSD = (pixel size · H) / f` |
| Relief displacement | `d = r·h / H` |

### Geodesy & state plane
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Geoid relationship | `H = h − N` |
| Elevation factor | `EF = R/(R + h)` (R ≈ 20,906,000 ft) |
| Combined factor | `CF = SF · EF` |
| Grid ↔ ground | `grid = ground · CF`;  `ground = grid / CF` |

### Business & economics
| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Single-payment future worth | `F = P·(1 + i)ⁿ` |
| Straight-line depreciation | `D = (C − S)/n` |
| Risk | `Risk = Hazard × Probability` |

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## Mixed exam-style problems (each combines topics)

These mirror how the FS chains concepts. Calculator keys are for the **TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991** (with **HP-35S** RPN noted). Set **DEGREE mode** first.

### A closed four-sided traverse

The figure shows the loop traverse used in Example 1: four legs by azimuth and distance returning to the start.

<svg width="460" height="360" viewBox="0 0 460 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ccc">
  <polygon points="120,300 370,155 250,55 0,200" fill="#eef4fb" stroke="#1c4e80" stroke-width="2" transform="translate(40,10)"/>
  <g transform="translate(40,10)">
    <circle cx="120" cy="300" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
    <circle cx="370" cy="155" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
    <circle cx="250" cy="55" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
    <circle cx="0" cy="200" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
    <text x="98" y="320" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">A</text>
    <text x="378" y="153" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">B</text>
    <text x="256" y="48" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">C</text>
    <text x="-14" y="200" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">D</text>
    <text x="235" y="240" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">AB: Az 30°, 500.00 ft</text>
    <text x="300" y="110" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">BC: Az 120°, 300.00 ft</text>
    <text x="95" y="110" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">CD: Az 210°, 500.00 ft</text>
    <text x="5" y="265" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">DA: Az 300°, 299.85 ft</text>
  </g>
</svg>

---

### Example 1 — Traverse → misclosure → relative precision → area (multi-step)

A four-leg closed loop A→B→C→D→A has azimuths/distances: **AB Az 30°00′, 500.00 ft; BC Az 120°00′, 300.00 ft; CD Az 210°00′, 500.00 ft; DA Az 300°00′, 299.85 ft.** Find the linear misclosure, the relative precision, and the enclosed area (acres) by coordinates. Start at A (N 5,000.00, E 5,000.00).

**Step 1 — latitudes & departures** (Lat = L·cos Az, Dep = L·sin Az):

| Leg | Az | L | Lat = L·cos Az | Dep = L·sin Az |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AB | 30° | 500.00 | +433.013 | +250.000 |
| BC | 120° | 300.00 | −150.000 | +259.808 |
| CD | 210° | 500.00 | −433.013 | −250.000 |
| DA | 300° | 299.85 | +149.925 | −259.678 |
| **Σ** | | **1,599.85** | **−0.075** | **+0.130** |

**Step 2 — linear misclosure:** `misclose = √(ΣLat² + ΣDep²) = √((−0.075)² + (0.130)²) = hypot(−0.075, 0.130) = 0.150 ft`.

**Step 3 — relative precision:** `= misclose / perimeter = 0.150 / 1,599.85 = 1/10,666`, i.e. about **1:10,700** — well within a normal traverse standard.

**Step 4 — coordinates** (running from A, before adjustment): B(N 5,433.01, E 5,250.00), C(N 5,283.01, E 5,509.81), D(N 4,850.00, E 5,259.81). **Step 5 — area by coordinates** (shoelace, X = E, Y = N) on the four corners gives **A ≈ 168,300 ft² ÷ 43,560 = 3.86 acres**.

**Calculator:** for each leg use **P▸R** — enter L, swap, enter Az, convert; read departure (ΔE) and latitude (ΔN) directly, accumulating with M+. The misclosure is one **R▸P** on (ΣLat, ΣDep): distance = 0.150 ft.

---

### Example 2 — Inverse + bearing reduction

From monument P (N 17,095.84, E 44,600.31) to monument Q (N 16,917.92, E 43,781.05), find the distance and azimuth.

1. `ΔN = 16,917.92 − 17,095.84 = −177.92`;  `ΔE = 43,781.05 − 44,600.31 = −819.26`.
2. **Distance:** `D = hypot(ΔN, ΔE) = √(177.92² + 819.26²) = 838.35 ft`.
3. **Azimuth:** both ΔN and ΔE negative → Quadrant III (SW). `Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN)` reduced to 0–360° = **257°45′** (bearing S 77°45′ W).

**Calculator (R▸P):** enter ΔE, swap, enter ΔN, **R▸P** → distance; swap → angle; if negative add 360°, then **▸DMS**.

---

### Example 3 — Error propagation on a measured area

A rectangular lot is measured **120.00 ± 0.04 ft** by **144.00 ± 0.05 ft**. State the area and its standard error.

1. **Area** = 120.00 × 144.00 = **17,280 ft²**.
2. **Propagate** (product rule): `σ_A = √[(L·σ_W)² + (W·σ_L)²] = √[(120·0.05)² + (144·0.04)²] = √(6.00² + 5.76²) = √(36.0 + 33.18) = √69.18 = ±8.3 ft²`.
3. **Result:** **17,280 ± 8.3 ft²**.

**Calculator:** `(120×.05)² ＋ (144×.04)²`, then `√`. This is the classic FS product-propagation item.

---

### Example 4 — Horizontal curve → stationing (multi-step)

A curve has **R = 1,000.00 ft**, **I = 36°00′**, with **PI at station 80+00.00**. Find T, L, and the stations of PC and PT.

1. **Tangent:** `T = R·tan(I/2) = 1,000·tan18° = 324.92 ft`.
2. **Length:** `L = R·I·(π/180) = 1,000·toRad(36°) = 628.32 ft`.
3. **PC = PI − T** = 8,000 − 324.92 = 7,675.08 → **station 76+75.08**.
4. **PT = PC + L** = 7,675.08 + 628.32 = 8,303.40 → **station 83+03.40** (add the **arc**, not T).

**Calculator:** `1000 × tan(18) =` → 324.92; `1000 × 36 × π ÷ 180 =` → 628.32. Sketch PI–PC–PT first so you never add T twice.

---

### Example 5 — Leveling + curvature/refraction

A backsight on BM-A (Elev 845.32 ft) reads **BS = 4.55 ft**; a foresight to TP-1 reads **FS = 6.78 ft**.

1. **HI** = Elev + BS = 845.32 + 4.55 = **849.87 ft**.
2. **Elev TP-1** = HI − FS = 849.87 − 6.78 = **843.09 ft**.
3. If the foresight were a long 0.30-mile shot (D = 1.58 thousand ft), the **(c & r)** correction ≈ 0.0206·D² = 0.0206·(1.58)² = **0.051 ft** (the rod reads high; subtract from the FS, raising the computed elevation).

**Calculator:** straight add/subtract; for (c & r), `0.0206 × 1.58² =`.

---

### Example 6 — Photo scale + grid-to-ground (mixed mapping/geodesy)

A vertical aerial photo is taken at flying height **H = 6,000 ft** above datum with a **152.4 mm (6 in) focal length** camera over terrain at **h = 1,200 ft**. A road measures **2.10 in** on the photo. (a) Find the photo scale; (b) the ground length; (c) if that ground length must be placed on the state-plane grid where **CF = 0.9999087**, find the grid length.

1. **Scale:** `S = f/(H − h) = 0.5 ft/(6,000 − 1,200) = 1/9,600`.
2. **Ground length:** photo 2.10 in × 9,600 = 20,160 in = **1,680.0 ft**.
3. **Grid length:** `grid = ground · CF = 1,680.0 × 0.9999087 = 1,679.85 ft`.

**Calculator:** scale denom `(6000−1200) ÷ 0.5 =` → 9,600; ground `2.10 × 9600 ÷ 12 =` → 1,680 ft; grid `× 0.9999087`.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## Final two-week study plan **Week 1 — shore up the heavy areas.** - **Days 1–2 — Boundary Law (Area 3, the largest).** Drill the **dignity of calls**, senior/junior rights, simultaneous-vs-sequential proration, PLSS restoration, easements, adverse possession, water boundaries. These are fast points — read, don't compute. - **Days 3–4 — Computations (Area 5).** Re-run traverse closure/adjustment, area by coordinates, both curve families, leveling, volumes. Do them on your real calculator until conversions are reflexive. - **Day 5 — Math & stats (Area 7).** Error propagation, std error of mean, weights, normal-curve probabilities. - **Days 6–7 — Take the full mock once, untimed, open-handbook.** Find your weak categories from the result. **Week 2 — simulate and polish.** - **Days 8–9 — Processes & Principles/Geodesy (Areas 1 & 4).** GNSS/OPUS, control, H = h − N, datums, Lambert vs TM, convergence. - **Day 10 — Mapping & Photogrammetry (Area 2).** Photo scale, GSD, relief displacement, vector/raster, contours, map types. - **Day 11 — Business & Ethics (Area 6).** Canons, responsible charge, contracts, E&O, safety, simple economics — light reading. - **Day 12 — Take the full mock TIMED and closed-book** (handbook only). Review every miss. - **Day 13 — Targeted review** of remaining weak categories; redo only missed problem types. - **Day 14 (day before) — Light only.** Skim the formula sheet, confirm logistics, sleep. ## Exam-day logistics - **Arrive early** at the Pearson VUE center with the required government **photo ID**; know the route (scope it the day before if you can). - Bring an **approved calculator** (TI-30X/36X, Casio fx-115/991, or HP 33s/35s) **with fresh batteries** — and ideally a backup of the same model. No programmable/graphing/CAS. - The center supplies scratch material; **no personal references** — the handbook and standards are on screen. - Eat beforehand; use the **25-min scheduled break** to reset. Earplugs/headphones are usually available. ## Time budget & test-taking mindset - **~2.9 minutes per question** (110 in 5 h 20 m). Most boundary/ethics/definition items take well under a minute — **bank those first**. - **Preview, then triage:** answer the quick recall items, **flag and skip** long COGO/curve problems, return to them with the time you banked. - **Each question tests only 1–2 concepts** — don't over-build. Use the simplest method (don't run a traverse routine to area a rectangle). - **Eliminate** obviously wrong choices; **sanity-check with coordinate sense** (if a point can't be SW of a known point, rule it out). - **Use the handbook as confirmation, not discovery** — know *where* each formula lives so you search, not hunt. - **Use all the time.** Reserve the last block to revisit every flagged item; never leave a question blank (no penalty for guessing). ## Final checklist
  • Approved calculator + backup, fresh batteries, DEGREE mode habit
  • Government photo ID matching your registration
  • Know the seven areas and that boundary + computations dominate
  • Reflexive on DMS↔decimal, R▸P / P▸R, statistics mode, store/recall
  • Master formula sheet internalized; handbook navigation practiced
  • At least one full timed, closed-book mock completed and reviewed
  • Logistics confirmed; rested
## How the handbook helps Every formula on the master sheet is in the NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.x — trig, triangles, **horizontal and vertical curves, photogrammetry, geodesy, EDM, area formulas, earthwork, probability & statistics**, plus economics and the ethics canons. Practice pulling each up by name so that on exam day the formula is a one-look confirmation and your minutes go to setting up the problem, not recalling the equation.

questions.question_text: The NCEES Fundamentals of Surveying exam consists of how many questions, and how is it administered?
options: 110 questions, computer-based (CBT) and closed-book with an electronic reference handbook
110 questions, open-book with any personal references allowed
questions.correct_answer: 110 questions, computer-based (CBT) and closed-book with an electronic reference handbook
questions.explanation: The FS is a 110-question CBT delivered at Pearson VUE. It is closed-book, but examinees get the electronic NCEES FS Reference Handbook (and supplied standards) on screen the entire exam; no personal references are allowed.

questions.question_text: On the FS, the total exam time (after the agreement and tutorial) is 5 hours 20 minutes for 110 questions. The approximate time budget per question is:
questions.explanation: 5 h 20 m = 320 minutes; 320 / 110 ≈ 2.9 minutes per question. Bank the quick boundary/ethics items and flag the long computations.

questions.question_text: Which knowledge area carries the single largest number of questions on the FS exam?
options: Boundary Law and Real Property Principles
questions.correct_answer: Boundary Law and Real Property Principles
questions.explanation: Boundary Law and Real Property Principles is the largest area (about 19–29 of the 110 questions), ahead of Survey Computations and Surveying Processes. The FS is as much a boundary exam as a math exam.

questions.question_text: In the hierarchy (dignity) of conflicting calls in a deed, which generally controls over the others?
questions.explanation: After senior rights and the written intent of the parties, calls for monuments control — natural monuments over artificial, and monuments over course and distance. Bearing controls over distance, and area (quantity) is the least reliable, controlling last.

questions.question_text: A line is measured 9 times with a standard deviation of a single measurement of 0.06 ft. The standard error of the mean is most nearly:
Point A is (N 1,500.00, E 2,000.00) and point B is (N 1,800.00, E 2,400.00). The horizontal distance A→B is most nearly:
The azimuth of the line from A(N 1,500.00, E 2,000.00) to B(N 1,800.00, E 2,400.00) is most nearly:
questions.explanation: Both ΔN (+300) and ΔE (+400) are positive → Quadrant I (NE). Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) = atan(400/300) = 53.13° = 53°08′.

questions.question_text: A closed traverse has a perimeter of 2,400.00 ft and a linear misclosure of 0.24 ft. Its relative precision is:
questions.explanation: Relative precision = misclosure/perimeter = 0.24/2,400 = 1/10,000.

questions.question_text: A backsight of 5.20 ft is taken on a benchmark at elevation 612.40 ft, then a foresight of 7.85 ft is read to a turning point. The turning-point elevation is:
A simple circular curve has R = 800.00 ft and a central angle I = 40°00′. The tangent distance T is most nearly:
For the curve with R = 800.00 ft and I = 40°00′, the curve (arc) length L is most nearly:
Which expression correctly relates orthometric height H, ellipsoidal height h, and geoid undulation N?
questions.explanation: Orthometric (elevation above the geoid) = ellipsoidal height − geoid undulation: H = h − N. GNSS gives h; subtract the geoid model N to get the elevation H.

questions.question_text: A ground distance of 4,000.00 ft is to be reduced to the state-plane grid using a combined factor of 0.9999200. The grid distance is most nearly:
questions.explanation: grid = ground × CF = 4,000.00 × 0.9999200 = 3,999.68 ft. Because the combined factor is less than 1, the grid distance is slightly shorter than the ground distance.

questions.question_text: A vertical aerial photo is taken at H = 5,000 ft above datum with a 6-in (0.5-ft) focal length camera over terrain at h = 1,000 ft. The photo scale is most nearly:
A four-corner lot has coordinates A(N 1,000, E 1,000), B(N 1,000, E 1,500), C(N 1,400, E 1,500), D(N 1,400, E 1,000). Its area in acres is most nearly:
questions.explanation: The lot is a 500 ft × 400 ft rectangle = 200,000 ft²; 200,000/43,560 = 4.59 acres. By the shoelace method the signed sum gives the same 200,000 ft².

questions.question_text: In a SEQUENTIAL conveyance, when the total of all called parcels does not exactly fit the parent tract, the excess or deficiency is:
options: absorbed entirely by the last (junior/remainder) parcel
always given to the senior parcel
questions.correct_answer: absorbed entirely by the last (junior/remainder) parcel
questions.explanation: Senior rights govern sequential conveyances: each earlier grantee gets the full called area, and the remaining (junior) parcel absorbs any excess or deficiency. Proration applies to SIMULTANEOUS conveyances (e.g., a recorded plat).

questions.question_text: Under the surveyor's ethical canons (NSPS / Model Rules), the surveyor's first and foremost obligation is to:
options: the safety, health, and welfare of the public
questions.correct_answer: the safety, health, and welfare of the public
questions.explanation: The Model Rules place duty to the public first and foremost. Surveyors also serve clients/employers honestly and deal fairly with other professionals, but public welfare comes first; sign and seal only work performed under your responsible charge and within your competence.

questions.question_text: The gradual, imperceptible build-up of land along a watercourse by natural deposition is called:
questions.explanation: Accretion is the slow, imperceptible addition of soil; the boundary moves with it. Avulsion is a sudden change (boundary stays fixed); reliction is land exposed by receding water; erosion is gradual loss.

questions.question_text: An equal-tangent vertical curve has g₁ = +1.0%, g₂ = −3.0%, L = 400 ft, with the PVC at elevation 250.00 ft. The elevation on the curve 100 ft past the PVC is most nearly:
A rectangular parcel is measured 200.00 ± 0.05 ft by 300.00 ± 0.05 ft. The standard error of the computed area is most nearly:
Which best describes an appurtenant easement?
options: a non-possessory right that runs with the land and passes to subsequent owners of the dominant tract
a possessory ownership interest in the servient tract
a right that automatically terminates upon sale of either parcel
a right that exists only if separately recorded each year
questions.correct_answer: a non-possessory right that runs with the land and passes to subsequent owners of the dominant tract
questions.explanation: An appurtenant easement benefits a dominant tract and burdens a servient tract; it is a non-possessory interest that runs with the land and conveys automatically with the dominant parcel. It is not separable from the land it benefits.

questions.question_text: The station of the PT of a simple circular curve is found by:
options: adding the curve length L to the PC station
adding the tangent distance T to the PC station
subtracting L from the PI station
questions.correct_answer: adding the curve length L to the PC station
questions.explanation: PT = PC + L, using the arc (curve) length. PC = PI − T. Adding the tangent T instead of the arc L is the most common curve-stationing blunder.

questions.question_text: True or False: On the FS exam, examinees may bring and use their own printed reference books and notes.
questions.explanation: False. The FS is closed-book. The only references are the on-screen electronic NCEES FS Reference Handbook and the supplied standards; no personal references are permitted.

questions.question_text: Two adjacent cross-sections 100 ft apart have cut end areas of 80 ft² and 160 ft². The average-end-area volume between them, in cubic yards, is most nearly:
True or False: A surveyor may sign and seal a plat prepared entirely by another firm that was not under the surveyor's responsible charge, as long as it appears correct.
questions.explanation: False. Under the ethical canons a surveyor may sign and seal only work performed by the surveyor or under the surveyor's direct responsible charge. Sealing another's work not under your charge violates the Model Rules.

problem_templates.name: Standard error of the mean
problem_templates.question_template: A distance is measured {{n}} times. The standard deviation of a single measurement is σ = {{sigma:f3}} ft. Compute the standard error of the mean (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: The standard error of the mean improves with the square root of the number of observations: σ_m = σ/√n = {{sigma:f3}}/√{{n}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Inverse distance between two points
problem_templates.question_template: Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}). Point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Compute the horizontal distance A→B (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Use rectangular→polar: enter ΔN and ΔE; the calculator returns distance = hypot(ΔN, ΔE) = {{_answer}} ft directly.

problem_templates.name: Inverse azimuth between two points
problem_templates.question_template: From point A (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}) to point B (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}), compute the azimuth of line A→B (decimal degrees, 0–360°).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN); add 360° if negative so the result lies in 0–360°. Here Az = {{_answer}}°. The signs of ΔN and ΔE place it in the correct quadrant automatically.

problem_templates.name: Forward coordinates from azimuth and distance
problem_templates.question_template: From point P (N {{nP:f2}}, E {{eP:f2}}), a line runs azimuth {{az:f2}}° for a distance of {{dist:f2}} ft to point Q. Compute the NORTHING of point Q (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: The latitude is ΔN = L·cos(Az), so N_Q = N_P + L·cos(Az) = {{nP:f2}} + {{dist:f2}}·cos({{az:f2}}°) = {{_answer}} ft (calc: P▸R, read the latitude component).

problem_templates.name: Area by coordinates (quadrilateral) → acres
problem_templates.question_template: A four-corner parcel has corners, in order, A (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}), B (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}), C (N {{nC:f2}}, E {{eC:f2}}), and D (N {{nD:f2}}, E {{eD:f2}}). Compute the enclosed area by coordinates (shoelace) and report it in ACRES.

solution_steps_template.title: Shoelace sum, halved, absolute value
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Sum the four cross-products, halve the absolute value → ft²

problem_templates.explanation_template: Apply the shoelace formula with X = Easting, Y = Northing, vertices in order A→B→C→D, take the absolute value, then divide by 43,560 ft²/acre. Area = {{_answer}} acres.

problem_templates.name: Horizontal curve tangent distance T
problem_templates.question_template: A simple circular curve has radius R = {{R:f2}} ft and deflection (central) angle I = {{I:f2}}°. Compute the tangent distance T (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: The tangent distance from the PI to the PC (and to the PT) is T = R·tan(I/2) = {{R:f2}}·tan({{I:f2}}°/2) = {{_answer}} ft. Set the calculator to DEGREE mode and use I/2.

problem_templates.name: Horizontal curve length L
problem_templates.question_template: A circular curve has radius R = {{R:f2}} ft and deflection angle I = {{I:f2}}°. Compute the curve (arc) length L (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: The arc length is L = R·I·(π/180) = {{R:f2}}·toRad({{I:f2}}°) = {{_answer}} ft. The PT is set by adding this arc length (not T) to the PC.

problem_templates.name: Differential leveling — new elevation
problem_templates.question_template: A backsight of {{bs:f2}} ft is taken on a benchmark at elevation {{elev:f2}} ft, then a foresight of {{fs:f2}} ft is read to a turning point. Compute the elevation of the turning point (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: HI = Elev + BS = {{elev:f2}} + {{bs:f2}} = {{hi:f2}} ft; then Elev = HI − FS = {{hi:f2}} − {{fs:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Vertical photo scale denominator
problem_templates.question_template: A vertical aerial photo is taken at flying height H = {{H:f1}} ft above datum with a focal length f = {{f:f3}} ft, over terrain at average ground height h = {{h:f1}} ft. Compute the scale denominator (the number S in 1:S).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Photo scale S = f/(H − h); the denominator is (H − h)/f = ({{H:f1}} − {{h:f1}})/{{f:f3}} = {{_answer}}, i.e. a scale of 1:{{_answer}}.

problem_templates.name: Grid distance from ground (combined factor)
problem_templates.question_template: A ground distance of {{ground:f2}} ft is to be reduced to the state-plane grid using a combined factor CF = {{cf:f7}}. Compute the grid distance (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: To go from ground to grid, multiply by the combined factor (CF = scale factor × elevation factor): grid = {{ground:f2}} × {{cf:f7}} = {{_answer}} ft. (To reverse, divide grid by CF.)

problem_templates.name: Average-end-area volume → cubic yards
problem_templates.question_template: Two adjacent cross-sections {{L:f1}} ft apart have end areas A₁ = {{a1:f1}} ft² and A₂ = {{a2:f1}} ft². Using the average-end-area method, compute the earthwork volume in CUBIC YARDS.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Average end area: V = L·(A₁+A₂)/2 = {{L:f1}}·({{a1:f1}}+{{a2:f1}})/2 = {{vft3:f1}} ft³. Divide by 27 ft³/yd³ → {{_answer}} yd³.

mock_questions.question_text: A line is measured 16 times with a single-measurement standard deviation of 0.08 ft. The standard error of the mean is most nearly:
A distance is measured with a relative precision of 1:25,000 over a 1,500-ft line. The expected positional uncertainty is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: Uncertainty = length × (1/precision) = 1,500 × (1/25,000) = 0.06 ft.

mock_questions.question_text: Under a normal distribution, approximately what percentage of observations fall within ±2 standard deviations of the mean?
mock_questions.explanation: The empirical (68-95-99.7) rule: about 68% lie within ±1σ, 95% within ±2σ, and 99.7% within ±3σ of the mean.

mock_questions.question_text: Point A is (N 2,000.00, E 3,000.00) and point B is (N 2,600.00, E 3,800.00). The distance A→B is most nearly:
The azimuth from A(N 2,000, E 3,000) to B(N 2,600, E 3,800) is most nearly:
A closed traverse has ΣLat = +0.12 ft and ΣDep = −0.16 ft. The linear misclosure is most nearly:
A simple circular curve has R = 1,200.00 ft and I = 30°00′. Its tangent distance T is most nearly:
A backsight of 6.40 ft is read on a benchmark at elevation 500.00 ft; the foresight to a turning point is 9.15 ft. The turning-point elevation is:
An equal-tangent vertical curve has g₁ = +2.0%, g₂ = −2.0%, L = 500 ft, PVC elevation 800.00 ft. The elevation at the midpoint of the curve (x = 250 ft) is most nearly:
A road measures 3.00 in on a 1:6,000 vertical aerial photo. Its ground length is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: Ground = photo distance × scale denominator = 3.00 in × 6,000 = 18,000 in = 1,500 ft.

mock_questions.question_text: A drone camera with 24 mm focal length and 5.0 µm pixels flies 120 m above ground. The ground sample distance is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: GSD = (pixel size × H)/f = (5.0 µm × 120 m)/24 mm. Using GSD_cm = (pixel_µm × H_m)/(f_mm × 10) = (5.0 × 120)/(24 × 10) = 600/240 = 2.5 cm.

mock_questions.question_text: On a topographic map, contour lines that are spaced very close together indicate:
mock_questions.explanation: Contours represent equal-elevation lines at a fixed interval; the closer they are, the greater the elevation change over a short horizontal distance, i.e., steeper ground. Widely spaced contours indicate gentle slopes.

mock_questions.question_text: In a GIS, the information describing the source, accuracy, projection, and date of a dataset is called:
mock_questions.explanation: Metadata is 'data about the data' — it documents lineage, accuracy, coordinate system, and currency, and is essential for responsibly sharing and using GIS datasets.

mock_questions.question_text: When two deed calls conflict, which of the following normally controls over the others?
options: a call for a natural monument
mock_questions.correct_answer: a call for a natural monument
mock_questions.explanation: In the dignity of calls, after senior rights and the parties' intent, natural monuments control over artificial monuments, and monuments control over course (bearing) and distance; area is least reliable.

mock_questions.question_text: Five lots are created at once by a single recorded subdivision plat, but the block is found to be 1.0 ft longer than the platted total. How is the excess handled?
options: prorated among the lots in proportion to their record dimensions
given entirely to the first lot
given entirely to the last lot
mock_questions.correct_answer: prorated among the lots in proportion to their record dimensions
mock_questions.explanation: Lots created simultaneously by a plat carry no seniority among them, so excess or deficiency is prorated proportionally. (In a sequential conveyance, by contrast, the junior/remainder parcel absorbs the difference.)

mock_questions.question_text: Title acquired by occupying land openly, notoriously, continuously, exclusively, and hostilely for the statutory period is acquired by:
mock_questions.explanation: Adverse possession transfers title when the possession satisfies the open, notorious, continuous, exclusive, and hostile elements for the jurisdiction's statutory period.

mock_questions.question_text: A right-of-way granted to a utility to cross another's land for power lines is an example of:
mock_questions.explanation: An easement is a non-possessory right to use another's land for a specific purpose (here, utility access). It does not convey ownership of the underlying land.

mock_questions.question_text: In the PLSS, a standard township is divided into how many sections?
mock_questions.explanation: A standard township is 6 miles square, divided into 36 sections of (nominally) one square mile (640 acres) each, numbered 1–36 in a boustrophedonic pattern beginning at the northeast corner.

mock_questions.question_text: A lost original PLSS corner that fell between two existing corners on a line is best restored by:
options: setting it at the record distance from the nearest corner only
placing it at the centroid of the section
GPS coordinates from the county GIS
mock_questions.explanation: A truly lost corner is restored by proportionate measurement — single proportion along a line, double proportion at an interior corner controlled in two directions — distributing the discrepancy in proportion to the record distances.

mock_questions.question_text: A warranty deed differs from a quitclaim deed in that the warranty deed:
options: guarantees the grantor's title against defects
conveys only whatever interest the grantor happens to have
is valid only if the land is surveyed
mock_questions.correct_answer: guarantees the grantor's title against defects
mock_questions.explanation: A warranty deed includes covenants guaranteeing good title and defending against claims. A quitclaim conveys only whatever interest (if any) the grantor holds, without warranties.

mock_questions.question_text: Land lost suddenly and perceptibly when a river changes course in a single flood event is governed by the doctrine of:
mock_questions.explanation: Avulsion is a sudden, perceptible change in a watercourse; the boundary remains at its former location. Accretion (gradual, imperceptible) moves the boundary with the slowly changing bank.

mock_questions.question_text: Which device achieves the highest absolute positional accuracy for establishing a geodetic control network?
options: static GNSS observations processed against CORS
mock_questions.correct_answer: static GNSS observations processed against CORS
mock_questions.explanation: Long static GNSS sessions post-processed against continuously operating reference stations (e.g., via OPUS) yield centimeter-level control, far better than autonomous or handheld positioning.

mock_questions.question_text: OPUS (Online Positioning User Service) is used to:
options: post-process a static GNSS occupation against the CORS network for accurate coordinates
mock_questions.correct_answer: post-process a static GNSS occupation against the CORS network for accurate coordinates
mock_questions.explanation: OPUS is an NGS service that processes a submitted static GNSS data file against nearby CORS stations and returns accurate NAD83 (and ellipsoidal/orthometric) coordinates for the occupied point.

mock_questions.question_text: Why must field notes and raw data files be kept complete, legible, and unaltered?
options: they are the original evidence of the survey and may be needed in court
they are required to be turned in to NCEES
they are only for billing purposes
mock_questions.correct_answer: they are the original evidence of the survey and may be needed in court
mock_questions.explanation: Field notes and raw data are the contemporaneous record of what was observed and are the surveyor's evidence of the work — they may be examined in litigation or retracement and must never be falsified.

mock_questions.question_text: For best accuracy when measuring a long distance with an EDM, the surveyor must account for:
options: atmospheric temperature and pressure (the ppm correction)
the color of the prism pole
mock_questions.correct_answer: atmospheric temperature and pressure (the ppm correction)
mock_questions.explanation: EDM measurements depend on the velocity of light through the atmosphere; temperature and pressure change the refractive index, requiring a parts-per-million correction (roughly 10 ppm per 10 °C or per 1 in Hg).

mock_questions.question_text: GNSS gives an ellipsoidal height h = 245.30 ft at a point where the geoid undulation N = −28.10 ft. The orthometric height (elevation) H is most nearly:
The geoid is best described as:
options: an equipotential surface of the Earth's gravity field, everywhere perpendicular to the plumb line
a perfect mathematical ellipsoid of revolution
the visible topographic surface of the Earth
the orbit plane of GPS satellites
mock_questions.correct_answer: an equipotential surface of the Earth's gravity field, everywhere perpendicular to the plumb line
mock_questions.explanation: The geoid is the equipotential (level) surface of gravity that the plumb line is perpendicular to everywhere; it is the reference for orthometric heights. The ellipsoid is the smooth mathematical reference used for horizontal datums.

mock_questions.question_text: For a long, narrow north-south state, the State Plane Coordinate System most commonly uses which projection?
mock_questions.explanation: Transverse Mercator is used for zones that extend predominantly north-south, while Lambert Conformal Conic is used for east-west zones. Both are conformal projections used by SPCS.

mock_questions.question_text: A grid distance of 5,280.00 ft is reduced from a ground distance using a combined factor of 0.9999000. The corresponding ground distance is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: ground = grid / CF = 5,280.00 / 0.9999000 = 5,280.53 ft. With CF < 1, the ground distance is slightly longer than the grid distance.

mock_questions.question_text: Under the surveyor's professional canons, a surveyor should accept assignments only when:
options: they fall within the surveyor's area of competence
mock_questions.correct_answer: they fall within the surveyor's area of competence
mock_questions.explanation: A core ethical canon requires practicing only within one's area of competence and signing/sealing only work performed under one's responsible charge, with the public's welfare paramount.

mock_questions.question_text: Which set of elements is required to form a valid contract?
options: a seal and a notary only
mock_questions.explanation: A valid contract generally requires offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity of the parties, and a lawful purpose. Scope of work and specifications define the deliverable but are not the bare formation elements.

mock_questions.question_text: Professional liability coverage that protects a surveyor against claims of negligent errors in professional work is commonly called:
mock_questions.explanation: Errors and omissions (professional liability) insurance covers claims arising from negligent acts, errors, or omissions in professional services — distinct from title insurance, which protects against defects in title.

mock_questions.question_text: A safety data sheet (SDS) under the GHS contains how many standardized sections?
mock_questions.explanation: An SDS has 16 sections in a fixed order; OSHA enforces sections 1–11 and 16 (sections 12–15 cover ecological, disposal, transport, and regulatory information not enforced by OSHA).
